company.name: Alder & Birch Legal, PLLC
company.tagline: Specialized data privacy counsel for fast-growing technology companies
differentiators: Attorneys who understand both regulatory landscape and the products clients are building
Built specifically for fast-growing tech companies who need privacy counsel before they can justify a full-time GC or CPO
Deep expertise across CCPA, GDPR, HIPAA, SOC 2, COPPA, and the growing patchwork of state privacy laws
Trusted by venture-backed startups from seed through Series C

services.name: Privacy Program Buildout
services.description: Designing and implementing privacy infrastructure from scratch — data mapping, privacy policies, cookie consent frameworks, data processing agreements, and internal governance procedures
services.best_for: Companies that have never formalized their privacy program and need to build one for enterprise sales, fundraising, or regulatory compliance

services.name: Regulatory Compliance
services.description: Achieving and maintaining compliance with CCPA/CPRA, GDPR, HIPAA, state privacy laws, and SOC 2 readiness from the legal side
services.best_for: Companies expanding into regulated markets, handling sensitive data, or facing enterprise procurement requirements

services.name: Incident Response
services.description: Guiding companies through data breaches — breach assessment, regulatory notification obligations, consumer notifications, and regulatory correspondence
services.best_for: Companies that have experienced or narrowly avoided a data breach and need immediate expert guidance

services.name: Product Counsel
services.description: Reviewing product features, data flows, and terms of service for privacy risk before launch — consent mechanisms, data retention, third-party sharing, children's privacy, AI/ML training data compliance
services.best_for: Companies launching new features that collect sensitive data or expanding into areas with privacy implications

common_triggers: Enterprise buyer requiring SOC 2, DPAs, or published privacy policies
EU/UK market expansion requiring GDPR compliance
Recent or narrowly avoided data breach
New state privacy law taking effect
First Head of Legal or compliance hire auditing existing gaps

sender.name: YOUR NAME
sender.title: Partner
sender.bio: A brief 1-2 sentence bio about the sender's background and expertise that builds credibility — e.g., '10 years advising venture-backed startups on data privacy, previously in-house counsel at a Series C healthtech company.'

tone: Consultative, not salesy — this is a partner reaching out as a peer, not a cold sales email
Concise — 4-6 sentences in the body, no walls of text
Specific — reference something real about the prospect's situation, never generic
Helpful — offer a concrete insight or observation, not just a meeting request
Professional but warm — this is a boutique firm, not BigLaw
structure: Subject line: Short, specific to the prospect's situation, no clickbait
Opening: One sentence that references a specific signal or fact about the prospect — this proves the email isn't spray-and-pray
Bridge: One sentence connecting that signal to a privacy/compliance need they likely have
Value: One sentence about how Alder & Birch has helped similar companies
Ask: A low-commitment CTA — typically a 15-minute call, not a sales demo
things_to_avoid: Never open with 'I hope this email finds you well' or similar filler
Never list all four service lines — lead with the one most relevant to their situation
Never mention the automated research or enrichment pipeline
Never use urgency tactics or false scarcity
Never include pricing in the cold email
Never be vague — 'companies like yours' is lazy, name their specific situation
Never write more than 150 words in the body
example_openings: Congrats on the Series B — that's a big milestone. As you scale the team and start closing enterprise deals, procurement teams are going to start asking for SOC 2 reports and DPAs that you probably don't have yet.

I saw that [Company] is hiring for a Head of Legal — that's usually the point where someone takes a hard look at the company's privacy posture and finds gaps. We work with a lot of companies at exactly this stage.
I noticed [Company] just launched [feature]. Processing [data type] at scale puts you squarely in HIPAA territory, and the penalties for getting it wrong are significant.
